methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
ACSL (Autonomous Systems & Connective Logistics) is a publicly listed drone and autonomous systems company based in Tokyo. The target operates in a sector with limited public comparables—no direct public peers in drones and autonomous systems could be identified—and a sparse private peer universe of five companies including Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, Elroy Air, and Terra Drone.

The peer-median valuation method was inapplicable to this analysis. The drones and autonomous sector contains only four same-sector peers globally, but none satisfied the filtering criteria for stage-matching or valuation-tier proximity required for reliable triangulation. Applying an all-sector median would introduce excessive methodological noise given the specialized capital requirements and revenue models endemic to autonomous systems development. As a result, conventional comparable-company analysis could not yield a defensible range.

This valuation exercise highlights a structural challenge: ACSL operates in a sector where the peer set remains too sparse and heterogeneous to support robust multiples-based valuation. A credible valuation would require either discounted cash flow modeling (if sufficient financial guidance exists) or precedent transaction analysis within autonomous systems M&A activity. Without these alternative methods applied here, no triangulated range can be supported. Key uncertainties include ACSL's revenue visibility, capex trajectory, and the degree to which Japanese public market multiples deviate from private funding-stage peers.
